metadata.notes: Content overhaul to Wingspan standard — Rogue B, March 2026. Sources: official Stonemaier rulebook (Essential Edition), BGG FAQ, r/boardgames strategy threads.

key_rulings.text: Your personal score is the LOWER of your two city scores. The player with the highest personal score wins.
Both partners must agree on where to place tiles in their shared city. If partners cannot agree, the player whose turn it is to break ties decides.
Shops score based on straight lines (same row or column): 1 shop = 2 pts, 2 = 5 pts, 3 = 10 pts, 4 = 15 pts, 5 = 20 pts.
Factories score based on ranking across ALL cities: most factories = 4 pts each, second-most = 3 pts each, third-most = 2 pts each, all others = 1 pt each.
Taverns score by number of different subtypes: 1 type = 1 pt, 2 types = 4 pts, 3 types = 9 pts, 4 types (complete set) = 17 pts.
Each house scores points equal to the number of OTHER unique tile types present in the city, not counting houses themselves.
Duplex tiles must be placed as a 1x2 domino (horizontal or vertical). Both halves go into the same city and cannot be split.
In Round 1, pass remaining tiles to the LEFT after each selection.
In Round 3, pass remaining tiles to the RIGHT after each selection.
If tied on personal score, the player whose higher-scoring city has more points wins.

subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Tiles
- **108 single building tiles** — Square tiles in 6 types, 18 of each:
  - **Shops** (blue) — Retail storefronts. Score by contiguous groups.
  - **Factories** (gray) — Industrial buildings. Score by competitive count across all cities.
  - **Parks** (green) — Natural green spaces. Score by contiguous groups.
  - **Taverns** (red) — Food and drink establishments. Come in 4 subtypes (music, drink, food, bed). Score by variety.
  - **Offices** (purple) — Professional buildings. Score by row/column clustering.
  - **Houses** (brown/yellow) — Residential dwellings. Score by diversity of other types in the city.
- **24 duplex tiles** — Larger tiles showing 2 building types. Each occupies 2 squares in the city grid. Used only in Round 2.

#### Markers & Reference
- **7 city tokens** — Placed between neighboring players to mark each shared city.
- **1 scoreboard** — Tracks final city scores (0-80 range).
- **7 scoring markers** — One per player, for the scoreboard.
- **7 player reference cards** — Double-sided. Show all 6 tile scoring rules. Keep one in front of you during the game.
- **1 seating randomizer card**

#### Automa (Solo/2-Player)
- **Automa tiles and reference card** — For solo and 2-player games. The Automa simulates extra players to make the drafting and dual-city mechanics work properly.

#### Other
- **1 rulebook** — Includes standard rules, solo rules, and Capitals expansion content (Essential Edition).

subtopics.title: Game Structure & Setup
subtopics.content:
#### How the Game Works
Between Two Cities is a **semi-cooperative** tile-drafting game played over 3 rounds. Each player builds **two cities simultaneously** — one shared with the neighbor to your left, one shared with the neighbor to your right. Each city is a **4×4 grid** of building tiles (16 squares total). At the end, your personal score is the **lower** of your two cities' scores.

#### Setting Up
1. **Seat players in a circle.** Randomly determine seating order.
2. **Place a city token** between each pair of neighboring players. Each token marks a shared city.
3. **Separate the tiles:** Put all 108 single building tiles in a face-down supply. Put all 24 duplex tiles in a separate face-down supply.
4. **Give each player** a scoring marker and a reference card.
5. **Place the scoreboard** where everyone can see it.

#### City Grid Layout
Each city is built as a **4×4 grid** (4 rows × 4 columns = 16 total squares). Single tiles fill 1 square. Duplex tiles fill 2 adjacent squares (placed as a 1×2 domino, horizontally or vertically). Once placed, tiles **cannot be moved**.

By the end of the game, each city receives: **5 single tiles** (Round 1) + **1 duplex tile** covering 2 squares (Round 2) + **5 single tiles** (Round 3) + **1 duplex tile** covering 2 squares (Round 2) = **14 squares from tiles**. The remaining 2 squares are filled by a combination of the tile counts that adjust per player count, ensuring all 16 squares are covered.

#### Checkpoint
Each player has a reference card and scoring marker. City tokens mark every shared city between neighbors. Single tiles and duplex tiles are in separate face-down supplies.

subtopics.title: Round-by-Round Flow
subtopics.content:
#### Round 1 — Single Tiles (Draft Left)
1. Each player draws **7 single tiles** from the supply and keeps them hidden in hand.
2. **Select 2 tiles** from your hand simultaneously — all players choose at the same time.
3. **Reveal** your 2 chosen tiles. Place **1 tile** in your left city and **1 tile** in your right city. Discuss placement with your city partners — you both need to agree on where each tile goes.
4. **Pass remaining tiles** (5 tiles) to the player on your **left**.
5. Repeat steps 2-4: pick 2 from the hand you receive, reveal, place, pass left.
6. Continue until tiles run out. On the final pick, you'll have 3 tiles left — pick 2, **discard the last 1 face-down**.

After Round 1, each city has **5 single tiles** placed in its 4×4 grid.

#### Round 2 — Duplex Tiles
1. Each player draws **3 duplex tiles** from the duplex supply.
2. **Select 2 duplex tiles** to keep. **Discard 1** face-down.
3. **Reveal** your 2 chosen duplex tiles. Place **1 duplex tile** in your left city and **1 duplex tile** in your right city. Remember: duplex tiles occupy 2 squares and must be placed as a horizontal or vertical domino.

After Round 2, each city has 5 single tiles + 2 duplex tiles (covering 4 squares) = **9 squares filled**.

#### Round 3 — Single Tiles (Draft Right)
1. Each player draws **7 single tiles** from the supply.
2. Same drafting process as Round 1, but pass remaining tiles to the **right** instead of the left.
3. After the final pick, discard 1 tile face-down.

After Round 3, each city is fully built at **16 squares** (the complete 4×4 grid).

#### Partnership Communication
You share each city with a partner. When placing tiles, **discuss openly** what the city needs, what scoring opportunities exist, and where tiles should go. Good communication is a core part of the game — not optional.

If partners cannot agree on placement, the player whose turn it is to break ties decides. Tie-breaking privilege alternates each round.

subtopics.title: Tile Scoring Rules
subtopics.content:
#### Shops (Blue) — Straight Line Scoring
Shops score based on **straight lines** of adjacent shops (same row or same column, not diagonal):
- 1 shop: **2 points**
- 2 shops in a line: **5 points**
- 3 shops in a line: **10 points**
- 4 shops in a line: **15 points**
- 5 shops in a line: **20 points** (possible with landscape mats)

If shops form an L, T, or + shape, each tile can only count toward **one** line — choose the most profitable grouping. Multiple separate lines each score independently.

#### Factories (Gray) — Competitive Ranking
Factories score based on **total count compared across ALL cities** in the game:
- City with the **most** factories (or tied for most): **4 points per factory**
- **Second-most** (or tied for second): **3 points per factory**
- **All others**: **2 points per factory**

If all cities are tied, all score 4 per factory. A city with 0 factories scores 0.

#### Parks (Green) — Connected Group Scoring
Parks score based on **connected groups** (orthogonally adjacent — sharing sides, not diagonals). Parks do NOT need to form a straight line; any connected cluster counts:
- 1 park: **2 points**
- 2 connected parks: **8 points**
- 3 connected parks: **12 points**
- 4 connected parks: **13 points**
- 5+ parks: **+1 point for each park beyond 3**

Multiple separate groups each score independently. Connect all your parks into one group if possible.

#### Taverns (Red) — Variety of Subtypes
Tavern tiles come in **4 subtypes** (music, drink, food, bed — each shown by a different icon). Taverns do NOT need to be adjacent. Points are based on how many **different subtypes** you have:
- 1 subtype: **1 point**
- 2 different subtypes: **4 points**
- 3 different subtypes: **9 points**
- All 4 subtypes (complete set): **17 points**

Multiple complete sets each score 17. Extra taverns beyond a complete set start a new set. Example: 2 music + 1 food + 1 drink + 1 bed = one set of 4 (17 pts) + one leftover (1 pt) = 18 pts.

#### Offices (Purple) — Cumulative Count + Tavern Bonus
Offices score based on **total count** in the city (they do NOT need to be adjacent):
- 1 office: **1 point**
- 2 offices: **3 points**
- 3 offices: **6 points**
- 4 offices: **10 points**
- 5 offices: **15 points**
- 6 offices: **21 points**

**Tavern adjacency bonus:** Each office adjacent to at least **1 tavern** scores **+1 bonus point**. An office can only gain this bonus once (even if next to multiple taverns), but one tavern can grant the bonus to multiple adjacent offices.

#### Houses (Brown/Yellow) — Diversity Multiplier
Each house scores points equal to the number of **other unique tile types** present in the city (not counting houses themselves). If your city has all 5 other types (shops, factories, parks, taverns, offices), each house is worth **5 points**. If your city only has 3 other types, each house is worth **3 points**.

**Factory penalty:** If a house is **adjacent to a factory**, that house scores only **1 point total** regardless of diversity. People don't like living next to factories — keep them separated!

Houses reward balanced city-building. Having at least 1 of every other type maximizes house value, but watch factory placement.

subtopics.title: Scoring & Endgame
subtopics.content:
#### When the Game Ends
The game always lasts exactly **3 rounds**. There is no variable end trigger — after Round 3, all cities are fully built and scoring begins.

#### Scoring Each City
Score each city independently by summing all 6 tile types:
1. **Shops** — Count straight lines, apply line-length scoring.
2. **Factories** — Compare factory counts across ALL cities, apply per-factory rank-based scoring.
3. **Parks** — Count connected groups, apply group-size scoring.
4. **Taverns** — Count unique subtypes, apply variety scoring.
5. **Offices** — Count total offices for cumulative scoring, plus tavern adjacency bonuses.
6. **Houses** — Multiply house count by number of other unique tile types (watch for factory adjacency penalty).

Sum all 6 category scores for the city's total.

#### Your Personal Score
Your personal score is the **LOWER** of your two city scores. This is the central tension — you must balance effort between both cities. A 40-40 split beats a 55-25 split.

#### Winning
The player with the **highest personal score** (highest lower city score) wins.

#### Tiebreakers
1. The tied player whose **higher-scoring city** has more points wins (rewarding balance).
2. If still tied, **more combined total points** across both cities wins.
3. If still tied, **shared victory**.

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### The Golden Rule: Balance Your Cities
Your score = the **lower** of your two cities. This changes everything:
- A 40-40 split (personal score: 40) beats a 55-25 split (personal score: 25).
- Always ask: **which city needs more help right now?**
- When picking 2 tiles, think about what **each** city needs — don't pick 2 tiles that only help one city.

#### Tile Priority Guide

**Houses are the safest bet.** As long as you maintain diversity (at least 1 of every other type), each house scores 5 points. Three houses in a diverse city = 15 easy points.

**Taverns need commitment.** A single tavern is a pitiful 1 point. Two different taverns jump to 4. A complete set of all 4 subtypes scores an explosive **17 points**. Commit to tavern variety or skip them.

**Shops want straight lines.** Place shops in the same row or column. 3 shops in a line = 10 points, but 3 isolated shops = only 6 points. Plan your line direction early and keep extending it.

**Parks want connected clusters.** Unlike shops, parks can connect in any shape (not just lines). A connected group of 3 parks = 12 points vs. 3 isolated parks = only 6.

**Factories are a gamble.** Having the most factories across all cities gives 4 points per factory — potentially massive. But if another city beats your count, your factories drop to 2-3 points each. Only invest heavily if you can win or tie for the top rank.

**Offices + Taverns synergize.** Offices scale well (up to 21 points for 6), and each office adjacent to a tavern gets +1 bonus. Build tavern-office clusters for reliable high scoring.

#### Drafting Tips
- **Watch what you pass.** Tiles you pass go to your neighbor — who shares a city with you. Passing good tiles to your left neighbor can directly help your shared left city.
- **Hate-draft sparingly.** Denying tiles to distant opponents rarely matters as much as building your own cities well.
- **In Round 1, establish a plan.** Your first 2-3 tile placements set the direction for each city. Decide early: is this city going heavy taverns? Heavy houses? Mixed?

#### Common Mistakes
- **Pouring all best tiles into one city.** Your neglected city drags your personal score down.
- **Scattering shops and parks.** Isolated tiles score poorly. Shops need straight lines; parks need connected groups.
- **Ignoring house diversity.** 4 houses with only 2 other tile types = 4×2 = 8 points. 4 houses with 5 other types = 4×5 = 20 points. A single office or factory tile can add 4 points to your houses.
- **Forgetting tavern subtypes.** 3 taverns of the **same** subtype still only score 1 point total. You need **different** subtypes.
- **Placing houses next to factories.** A house adjacent to a factory scores only 1 point instead of potentially 5. Keep them apart!
- **Not communicating with partners.** Talk about what the city needs, what's coming in the draft, and where tiles should go. Silence leads to bad cities.

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### Your First Game of Between Two Cities

**Setup**: Sit in a circle. You have a city shared with your left neighbor and a city shared with your right neighbor. Keep your reference card visible — it shows all 6 tile scoring rules.

**Round 1, Pick 1**: Draw 7 single tiles. Look at them. Identify which tile types you have. Pick 2 tiles — one for your left city, one for your right city. Everyone reveals simultaneously. Discuss placement with your partners and place tiles in the 4×4 grids.

**Key first-pick tip**: Place a house in each city if possible. Houses are versatile — they'll score well as long as you diversify later. If you see 2 taverns of different subtypes, grab both — that's 4 points instead of 2 points for a single tavern.

**Round 1 continues**: Pass remaining tiles left. Pick 2 more, place 1 in each city. Repeat. After 3 picks (6 tiles placed, 3 in each city), you'll have 1 tile left — discard it face-down. Then pick 2 from the hand you receive, discard 1. Each city now has 5 tiles.

**Round 2 — Duplex**: Draw 3 duplex tiles. Each shows 2 building types. Keep 2, discard 1. Place 1 duplex in each city. Think about what types each city needs — a Shop/House duplex can add both a cluster piece and diversity at once.

**Round 3**: Draw 7 new tiles. Same process as Round 1, but pass **right**. This is your last chance to fill scoring gaps. Need a 4th tavern subtype? Desperately need a park to connect two groups? Prioritize accordingly.

**Scoring**: Score each city type by type using your reference card. Add up all 6 categories per city. Your personal score is the **lower** city. First-game target: 40-50 points is good. 55+ is excellent.

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### The Partner Paradox

Your score equals your LOWER-scoring city. This means you need both cities to perform well — it's useless to have a 60-point city and a 30-point city. Always invest in whichever city is weaker.

#### Tile Valuation

**Factories**: Scale with adjacency — cluster them. One isolated factory is worth 2 pts; four in a block are worth 16+ pts total. **Shops**: Need all 4 types for maximum bonus. **Taverns**: Set collection — 1/4/9/17 points for 1/2/3/4 unique types. **Houses**: Score based on OTHER building types present, so they're strongest in diverse cities.

#### Draft Reading

Track what your neighbors are drafting. If left-neighbor is hoarding factories, feed their shared city factories too (you both benefit). If right-neighbor ignores taverns, don't invest in taverns for your shared city.

#### Duplex Strategy

Duplex tiles (placed across 2 spaces) are high-value but inflexible. Draft them early when you have space to place them optimally. Late-game duplexes often end up in suboptimal positions.

#### The Kingmaker Position

In the final round, look at the scoreboard. If you can't win, choose tile placements that don't kingmake a specific opponent. Balanced play earns trust for future games.
